title: Topic 107: The Psychology of Gacha Pity Systems: Guaranteed Disappointment
content:
### ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the paradoxical world of gacha game 'pity systems,' a mechanic ostensibly designed to soothe the sting of digital misfortune, yet masterfully engineered to guarantee a peculiar form of disappointment. Through a multidisciplinary lens encompassing behavioral economics, psychology, and a healthy dose of gallows humor, we explore how these systems exploit cognitive biases such as the sunk cost fallacy and variable-rate reinforcement schedules, transforming the pursuit of virtual treasures into a Sisyphean task with a credit card. By analyzing popular gacha titles and the lamentations of their player bases, we argue that the 'pity' offered is not one of compassion, but a carefully calculated transaction designed to keep players teetering on the precipice of hope and despair. The guaranteed reward, we contend, is not the coveted five-star hero, but the gnawing certainty that true satisfaction is always just one more pull away.
### INTRODUCTION

In the sprawling, pixelated landscapes of modern gaming, a peculiar phenomenon has taken root, captivating millions and generating billions. This is the world of gacha games, a digital realm where fortune favors the persistent, the profligate, and the profoundly patient. At the heart of this burgeoning industry lies a mechanism as ingenious as it is insidious: the pity system. Presented as a benevolent safety net for the perpetually unlucky, the pity system promises a guaranteed high-value reward after a specific number of failed attempts. It is, in essence, a digital pat on the back, a comforting whisper of "there, there, better luck next time... but not *too* much better."

This paper, however, posits a more cynical, and arguably more accurate, interpretation. We contend that the pity system is not a gesture of goodwill, but a masterstroke of psychological manipulation, a meticulously crafted engine of "guaranteed disappointment." It is a system that dangles the carrot of certainty in a field of random-number-generated sticks, ensuring that players remain ensnared in a loop of hope, despair, and, most importantly, expenditure. We will explore how the very guarantee of a reward, paradoxically, diminishes its value and perpetuates a cycle of desire. Through an examination of the psychological principles that underpin these systems, from the Skinner Box to the sunk cost fallacy, we will dissect the architecture of this digital Skinner box. We will also present a series of real-world examples, drawn from the annals of gaming history and the tear-stained forums of disillusioned players, to illustrate the profound and often comical impact of these systems on human behavior. Ultimately, this paper seeks to answer a simple question: in the world of gacha games, who is really being pitied? The player, or the game developer laughing all the way to the bank?
### LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has been somewhat slow to catch up to the gacha phenomenon, preferring to study more traditional forms of gambling and addiction. However, a growing body of work is beginning to emerge from the digital trenches, as researchers and cultural critics alike start to unpack the intricate web of psychological manipulation that underpins these games. This review will synthesize the existing literature, with a particular focus on the concepts of "dark patterns," behavioral economics, and the psychology of gambling, all of which provide a fertile ground for understanding the paradoxical nature of the pity system.

The term "dark patterns," as popularized by Harry Brignull, refers to user interfaces designed to trick users into doing things they didn't mean to, like buying insurance with their airline ticket or signing up for recurring bills. While Brignull's work was not initially focused on gaming, the concept has been enthusiastically adopted by critics of the gacha model. Websites like *darkpattern.games* have sprung up to catalogue the various ways in which games exploit user psychology, from "playing by appointment" mechanics that demand daily logins to the "social pyramid schemes" that encourage players to recruit their friends. The pity system, in this context, can be seen as a particularly sophisticated dark pattern, one that masquerades as a user-friendly feature while subtly encouraging continued engagement and spending.

From the field of behavioral economics, we draw upon the foundational work of Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, whose research on cognitive biases provides a powerful framework for understanding player behavior. The **sunk cost fallacy**, for instance, is on full display as players, having already invested significant time and money into a game, feel compelled to continue spending in order to justify their past investment. The pity system preys on this fallacy by offering a tangible, albeit often disappointing, reward for this continued investment. **Loss aversion**, the principle that the pain of losing is psychologically twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining, is another key concept. The pity system mitigates the pain of repeated losses with the promise of an eventual win, but the win itself is often not enough to offset the accumulated frustration.

Finally, we turn to the psychology of gambling, a field with a long and storied history of studying the allure of random rewards. The work of B.F. Skinner and his famous "Skinner box" experiments are particularly relevant here. Skinner demonstrated that pigeons, when rewarded with food pellets on a **variable-rate reinforcement schedule** (i.e., at random intervals), would peck at a lever more compulsively than when rewarded on a fixed schedule. Gacha games are, in essence, digital Skinner boxes, and the pity system is a clever twist on this classic experiment. It introduces a fixed-rate reinforcement schedule (the guaranteed pity reward) into a system that is otherwise dominated by variable-rate reinforcement, creating a complex and highly addictive cocktail of hope and expectation.
### ANALYSIS

The pity system, at its core, is a masterclass in psychological manipulation, a finely tuned instrument designed to extract maximum engagement and revenue from its subjects. It operates on a simple, yet devastatingly effective, principle: the illusion of control in a system that is, by its very nature, chaotic. By offering a guaranteed reward, the pity system provides a semblance of predictability in the otherwise unpredictable world of gacha pulls. This, in turn, fosters a sense of agency in the player, a belief that their continued efforts will, eventually, be rewarded. It is a classic case of what psychologists call the **illusion of control**, the tendency for people to overestimate their ability to control events. In the context of gacha games, this illusion is carefully cultivated by the game developers, who understand that a player who feels in control is a player who is more likely to keep playing, and more importantly, keep paying.

The pity system also preys on our innate desire for closure, a psychological phenomenon known as the **Zeigarnik effect**. This effect, named after Soviet psychologist Bluma Zeigarnik, posits that people remember uncompleted or interrupted tasks better than completed tasks. In the world of gacha, the "task" is the acquisition of a desired character or item. The pity system, by guaranteeing an eventual reward, ensures that this task is never truly completed. Even after receiving the pitied prize, there is always another, more desirable prize on the horizon, another limited-time banner, another chance to spin the digital roulette wheel. This perpetual state of incompletion keeps players mentally invested in the game, constantly thinking about their next pull, their next chance to finally "complete" their collection.

Furthermore, the pity system is a powerful tool for fostering a sense of **endowment**. The **endowment effect**, a concept from behavioral economics, describes our tendency to value things more highly simply because we own them. In gacha games, the pity system ensures that players will, eventually, "own" a high-value item. This ownership, however fleeting, creates a psychological attachment to the item, and by extension, to the game itself. The player who has just received a five-star character through the pity system is not just a player; they are the proud owner of a rare and valuable digital asset. This sense of ownership makes it that much harder to walk away from the game, to abandon the collection that they have so painstakingly, and expensively, assembled.

Finally, the pity system is a brilliant application of **prospect theory**, another gem from the minds of Kahneman and Tversky. Prospect theory tells us that people make decisions based on the potential value of losses and gains rather than the final outcome. The pity system is a master of framing. It frames the gacha experience not as a series of losses with the occasional win, but as a journey towards a guaranteed reward. Each pull that does not yield the desired prize is not a failure, but a step closer to the pity threshold. This reframing of the experience is crucial. It transforms what would otherwise be a frustrating and demoralizing experience into a hopeful and even exciting one. The player is not just gambling; they are making progress, they are working towards a goal. And that, in the end, is the true genius of the pity system.
### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The theoretical underpinnings of the pity system are all well and good, but to truly appreciate its insidious genius, we must turn to the real world, to the digital salt mines where players toil in the hopes of striking gacha gold. The annals of gaming are replete with tales of triumph and tragedy, of fortunes spent and dreams dashed, all in the pursuit of that elusive five-star prize.

One of the most infamous examples of the pity system in action can be found in the popular gacha game *Genshin Impact*. The game features a pity system that guarantees a five-star character at 90 pulls. However, there is a catch. The guaranteed five-star character is not necessarily the limited-time character featured on the banner. There is a 50/50 chance that the player will receive a standard, non-limited character instead. This "50/50" system has become a source of endless frustration and memes within the *Genshin Impact* community. Players who "lose the 50/50" are forced to make another 90 pulls to guarantee the limited-time character, a soul-crushing and expensive proposition. The emotional rollercoaster of winning or losing the 50/50 is a powerful driver of engagement, as players share their tales of woe and triumph on social media, creating a sense of shared community and, of course, free advertising for the game.

Another illustrative case study can be found in the world of *Arknights*, a tower defense gacha game with a particularly unforgiving pity system. In *Arknights*, the pity system only guarantees a six-star operator (the highest rarity) after 50 pulls *without* a six-star. This means that a player could theoretically go on a long streak of pulls without receiving a six-star, only to have their pity broken by a non-featured, or "off-banner," operator. The *Arknights* community is filled with stories of players who have saved up for months to pull for a specific operator, only to have their pity broken by a character they already have, or worse, a character they have no interest in. This system, while seemingly more generous than *Genshin Impact*'s, is in many ways more cruel, as it offers no guarantee of ever receiving the desired character.

These are just two examples, but the basic principles are the same across the gacha landscape. From *Honkai: Star Rail* to *Wuthering Waves*, the pity system is a ubiquitous feature, a constant reminder that in the world of gacha, the house always wins. The stories of players who have spent thousands of dollars to obtain a single character are not outliers; they are the logical conclusion of a system designed to exploit human psychology for profit. The pity system, in this context, is not a safety net; it is a spider's web, and the players are the flies.
### DISCUSSION

The implications of the pity system extend far beyond the realm of gaming. The techniques of psychological manipulation that have been perfected in the digital world are increasingly being deployed in other areas of our lives, from social media to online shopping. The gamification of everything, it seems, is upon us, and the pity system is a harbinger of what is to come.

At its heart, the pity system is a tool for manufacturing desire. It is a machine for turning wants into needs, for transforming casual players into committed consumers. It does this by creating a perpetual state of dissatisfaction, a constant yearning for the next reward, the next dopamine hit. This is a dangerous game to play, not just for our wallets, but for our mental health. The constant cycle of hope and disappointment can lead to feelings of anxiety, depression, and even addiction. The very term "gacha addiction" is now a recognized phenomenon, with support groups and therapy programs springing up to help those who have fallen victim to the allure of the digital Skinner box.

But the pity system is not just a story about individual pathology; it is also a story about corporate greed. The gacha industry is a multi-billion dollar behemoth, and the pity system is one of the key drivers of its profitability. The fact that these systems are often targeted at children and young adults, who are particularly vulnerable to their manipulative charms, raises serious ethical questions. Are we comfortable with a world in which our children are being taught to gamble from a young age, to equate happiness with the acquisition of virtual goods? These are questions that we, as a society, need to start asking.

The pity system, then, is more than just a game mechanic; it is a cultural artifact, a reflection of our times. It is a testament to the power of technology to shape our behavior, to exploit our weaknesses, and to turn our desires into profit. It is a cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked capitalism, of a world in which everything, even our own happiness, is for sale.
### CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the gacha pity system, far from being a benevolent feature designed to aid the player, is a sophisticated and highly effective tool of psychological manipulation. It is a system that preys on our cognitive biases, our innate desires, and our deepest insecurities, all in the service of profit. It is a system that guarantees not satisfaction, but a perpetual state of wanting, a never-ending cycle of hope and disappointment. The pity, it seems, is not for the player, but for the society that allows such a system to exist.

As we move further into the digital age, it is imperative that we become more critical consumers of the technologies that we use. We must learn to recognize the dark patterns that are designed to exploit us, to resist the siren song of the digital Skinner box. The pity system is a wake-up-call, a reminder that the games we play are not always just games. Sometimes, they are playing us.

5.  Skinner, B. F. (1953). *Science and human behavior*. The Macmillan Company.
6.  Tang, A. C. Y., Lee, P. H., Lam, S. C., Siu, S. C. N., & Ye, C. J. (2022). Prediction of problem gambling by demographics, gaming behavior and psychological correlates among gacha gamers: A cross-sectional online survey in Chinese. *Frontiers in Psychiatry*, 13, 940281.
